skill.name: omc
skill.description: oh-my-claudecode is a Claude Code orchestration guide for multi-agent team workflows, autonomous execution modes, verification loops, and optional cross-validation tools.
skill.summary: Coordinate Claude Code multi-agent workflows with OMC team, autopilot, Ralph, and ultrawork modes.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static findings for weak cryptography appear to be false positives caused by prose and command examples, with no cryptographic primitive shown. The skill still presents high risk because it directs installation of an external community plugin, enables broad Claude Code tools, modifies a hidden home-directory settings file, and configures external notification endpoints.

high_findings.title: External Community Plugin Installation
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add and install a plugin from an external GitHub repository. This is a supply-chain risk because the installed plugin code is outside this skill report and can change independently.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The installation command and GitHub URL are explicit. I did not find proof of malicious code in these files, but the external install path creates material supply-chain exposure.

high_findings.title: Broad Agent Tool Access With Autonomous Execution
high_findings.description: The skill advertises Bash and Write access and promotes autonomous or persistent execution modes. This can amplify impact if the external plugin or agent workflow performs unsafe actions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools and autonomous execution claims are directly present. The risk depends on runtime behavior not included in the scanned files, so confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory Configuration Change
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to enable native teams in a hidden Claude settings file under the home directory. This is a legitimate configuration step, but it changes persistent user environment state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden settings path and environment flag are explicit. The change appears documented and user-directed, so it is a moderate operational risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: External Notification Callback Configuration
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Telegram and Discord callback setup with bot tokens, chat identifiers, and webhook URLs. Misuse could expose workflow metadata or secrets to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The callback commands and secret-like parameters are directly shown. No evidence shows automatic exfiltration, but the documented integration sends data to external endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-crypto findings appear to match prose, mode names, or Markdown content. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or another weak cryptographic primitive being used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are documentation and structured summaries, not crypto code. Manual review found no cryptographic algorithm invocation at the reported locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The skill includes hardcoded GitHub and documentation URLs. These are expected for installation and documentation, but users should verify the destination before installing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and serve documentation or installation purposes. They are not hidden exfiltration endpoints in the scanned files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Like Installation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation contains commands that install plugins and global packages. These should be reviewed before execution because they can fetch and run code outside this repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit installation instructions. They are user-directed examples, not automatically executed by the scanned files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Local Configuration Target
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references a hidden Claude Code settings file and an environment flag. Persistent configuration changes can alter future agent behavior beyond one session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The home-directory path and setting are explicit. The instruction is transparent, so it is a controlled risk rather than stealthy filesystem access.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Service Callback Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands for Telegram and Discord callbacks. These integrations require careful token handling and destination verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands include token and webhook placeholders. No hidden network code is present, but external callback configuration is a real exposure point.

content.user_title: Orchestrate Claude Code Agents With OMC
content.value_statement: Complex Claude Code work often needs planning, execution, verification, and follow-up across several agents. OMC provides documented workflows for team, autopilot, Ralph, and ultrawork orchestration modes.
actual_capabilities: Documents installation steps for the oh-my-claudecode plugin.
Explains Team, Autopilot, Ultrawork, Ralph, Pipeline, Swarm, and Ultrapilot modes.
Lists magic keywords that route natural language requests to orchestration modes.
Shows commands for setup, updates, diagnostics, waiting, and notifications.
Describes optional Gemini CLI and Codex CLI cross-validation workflows.
Provides quick examples for parallel execution and verify-fix loops.
limitations: The scanned files are documentation and do not include the external plugin implementation.
The skill requires Claude Code and the external oh-my-claudecode plugin to perform orchestration.
Notification integrations require user-provided tokens or webhook URLs.
Autonomous execution can make broad changes if users grant powerful tools.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Feature Delivery
use_cases.description: Use Team mode to plan, implement, verify, and fix a feature with multiple specialized agents.

use_cases.title: Automate Repetitive Fixes
use_cases.description: Use Autopilot or Ultrawork to handle lint, TypeScript, or refactor tasks across a repository.

use_cases.title: Add Review Redundancy
use_cases.description: Use optional Gemini CLI or Codex CLI checks to cross-validate architecture and code quality decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Start Team Mode
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for one clear engineering task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OMC team mode with three executor agents to fix all TypeScript errors and verify the result.

prompt_templates.title: Run Autopilot
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for a contained feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OMC autopilot to build a REST API for managing tasks, including tests and final verification.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Before Execution
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow when requirements need refinement.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OMC planning mode to interview me about the feature, write a plan, then wait for approval.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Cross-Validation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for high-impact code changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OMC team mode, then request Codex CLI architecture validation before applying final fixes.

output_examples.input: Fix all TypeScript errors with OMC team mode.
output_examples.output: A staged workflow plan, parallel executor assignments, verification results, and a final summary of fixed errors.

output_examples.input: Build a task management REST API with autopilot.
output_examples.output: A completed implementation path covering planning, endpoints, tests, verification, and any follow-up fixes.

output_examples.input: Use Ralph mode to refactor authentication until complete.
output_examples.output: A persistent execution loop that plans the refactor, applies changes, verifies behavior, and resolves remaining issues.

best_practices: Review external plugin source and version before installing or updating.
Run autonomous modes in a clean branch with clear task boundaries.
Use notification tokens and webhook URLs only after verifying destinations.
anti_patterns: Do not run persistent autonomous modes on sensitive repositories without supervision.
Do not paste production secrets into callback configuration commands.
Do not install optional global CLI tools unless the workflow requires them.

faq.question: What is OMC?
faq.answer: OMC is documentation for using oh-my-claudecode multi-agent orchestration workflows inside Claude Code.

faq.question: Does this skill include the plugin code?
faq.answer: No. The scanned skill files document how to install and use an external community plugin.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The skill directs external plugin installation, broad agent execution, hidden settings changes, and optional external callbacks.

faq.question: Are the weak cryptography alerts confirmed?
faq.answer: No. Manual review found no weak cryptographic primitive in the scanned documentation files.

faq.question: Can I use it without Gemini CLI or Codex CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill says those tools are optional and used for additional cross-validation.

faq.question: What should I check before use?
faq.answer: Review the external plugin repository, confirm permissions, use a branch, and protect tokens used for callbacks.
